summary: PSM-level FDR only — protein-level FDR missing against inflated database. MBR enabled without FDR control for transferred identifications. No multiple testing correction in differential expression. Three compounding FDR failures.

findings.title: Only PSM-level FDR applied — protein-level FDR absent
findings.message: search_config.yaml applies FDR at PSM level only (psm_fdr: 0.01). No peptide-level or protein-level FDR is configured. With a custom proteogenomics database of 65,200 entries (3.2x reference), PSM-level-only FDR substantially underestimates the false positive rate at the protein level. Expected 5-10% false positive protein rate at PSM 1% threshold against this database.
findings.impact: Protein list inflated with false positive identifications. Variant proteins disproportionately affected. Combined with database inflation (see proteogenomics-reviewer PG-2), actual variant-class FDR is approximately 3.2% at nominal 1%.
findings.recommendation: Enable protein-level FDR: set protein_fdr: 0.01 in MaxQuant or equivalent. For proteogenomics, implement class-specific FDR (reference vs variant class) per Nesvizhskii 2014.
findings.code_snippet:
fdr_control:
  psm_fdr: 0.01
  peptide_fdr: null  # NOT SET
  protein_fdr: null  # NOT SET

findings.title: Match-Between-Runs enabled without FDR control for transferred identifications
findings.message: MBR is enabled with a 0.7-minute retention time window. MBR-transferred identifications carry unknown false discovery probability — no established FDR framework exists for MBR. For NMD-susceptible phantom proteins (see proteogenomics-reviewer PG-3), MBR can create artificial consistency: a phantom peptide observed by chance in one run is transferred to all other runs as if it were a real identification.
findings.impact: MBR-only proteins (no MS/MS evidence in that sample) are included in differential expression. Phantom proteins from NMD-susceptible sequences may appear consistently across runs via MBR, producing false signals in DE analysis with artificially low variance.
findings.recommendation: Flag MBR-only identifications using 'Only identified by site' column. Exclude MBR-only proteins from differential expression or apply stringent quant thresholds (require MS/MS in at least 50% of samples per condition).
findings.code_snippet:
mbr:
  enabled: true
  match_time_window: 0.7
  # No MBR FDR control or MBR-only exclusion from DE

findings.title: No multiple testing correction applied in differential expression analysis
findings.message: differential_expression.R applies limma-voom t-tests across all proteins but uses raw p-values (p.value < 0.05) for significance calling rather than FDR-adjusted values (adj.p.value). With ~4,500 proteins tested, the expected number of false positives at p < 0.05 without correction is ~225 proteins purely by chance.
findings.impact: Differential expression results are fundamentally unreliable. At ~4,500 proteins tested with alpha=0.05 and no correction, ~225 false positives are expected. Compound effect with MBR-included proteins and inflated database: the false discovery rate in the DE protein list may exceed 20%.
findings.recommendation: Apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction: use adj.P.Val from limma topTable() with a 5% FDR threshold. This is standard practice and is already computed by limma — the code simply needs to reference adj.P.Val instead of P.Value.
findings.code_snippet:
# differential_expression.R line 88
de_proteins <- results[results$P.Value < 0.05, ]  # RAW p-value
# Should be:
# de_proteins <- results[results$adj.P.Val < 0.05, ]
references: Benjamini Y, Hochberg Y (1995) Controlling the false discovery rate: a practical and powerful approach to multiple testing. JRSS-B 57(1):289-300
